docs.title: Documentation for pynxtools-xrd
docs.text:
pynxtools-xrd is a free and open-source data software for harmonizing X-ray diffraction data and metadata for research data management using NeXus, implemented with the goal to make scientific research data FAIR (findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable).

pynxtools-xrd, which is a plugin for pynxtools, provides a tool for reading data from .xrdml files that were obtained with PANalytical X'Pert PRO version 1.5 (instruments). The tool standardizes these data such that it is compliant with the NeXus application definitions NXxrd_pan. pynxtools-xrd is developed both as a standalone reader and as a tool within NOMAD, which is the open-source data management platform for materials science we are developing with FAIRmat.

pynxtools-xrd is useful for scientists from the X-ray powder diffraction community that wish to explore standardization of their data using NOMAD.

Contact

For questions or suggestions:

  • Open an issue on the pynxtools-xrd GitHub
  • Join our Discord channel
  • Get in contact with our lead developers.
Project and community
  • NOMAD code guidelines

The work is funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) - 460197019 (FAIRmat).

docs.title: Get in contact
docs.text:
NOMAD, pynxtools, and pynxtools-xrd are open source projects that warmly welcome community projects, contributions, suggestions, bug fixes, and constructive feedback. pynxtools is built mainly within FAIRmat Area B - Experiment.

You can reach us through different channels. You can send an email directly to one of the main contributors:

Name e-mail Github profiles Rubel Mozumder e-mail @RubelMozumder Dr. Markus Kühbach e-mail @mkuehbach Dr. Lukas Pielsticker e-mail @lukaspie

Alternatively, you can also:

  • Open an issue on GitHub
  • Join the NOMAD discord channel and ask us there directly.

docs.title: Development guide
docs.text: This tutorial will guide you through on how to set up a working environment for developing pynxtools-xrd.

docs.title: What should you know before this tutorial?
docs.text:
• You should read the guide on getting started with pynxtools.
  • You should read the installation tutorial.

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text:
You will know

  • how to setup your environment for developing pynxtools-xrd
  • how to make changes to the software
  • how to test the software
  • how to contribute on GitHub
  • how to use pynxtools-xrd as a NOMAD plugin

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Structure of the pynxtools-xrd repository

The software tools are located inside src/pynxtools_xrd. They are shipped with unit tests located in tests.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
It is recommended to use python 3.12 with a dedicated virtual environment for this package. Learn how to manage python versions and virtual environments. We recommend using uv, an extremely fast Python package and project manager. In this tutorial, you will find parallel descriptions, using either uv or a more classical approach using venv and pip.

Start by creating a virtual environment:

uvvenv

uv is capable of creating a virtual environment and install the required Python version at the same time.

uv venv --python 3.12

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

python -m venv .venv

That command creates a new virtual environment in a directory called .venv.

docs.title: Development installation
docs.text:
We start by cloning the repository:

git clone https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-xrd.git \\
    --branch main \\
    --recursive pynxtools-xrd
cd pynxtools-xrd

Next, we install the package in editable mode (together with its dependencies):

uvpip 
uv pip install -e ".[dev]"

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Linting and formatting
docs.text:
We are using ruff and mypy for linting, formatting, and type checking. It is recommended to use the pre-commit hook available for ruff which formats the code and checks the linting before making a Git commit.

Install the precommit by running

pre-commit install

from the root of this repository.

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
There exist unit tests for the software written in pytest which can be used as follows:

pytest -sv tests

docs.title: Editing the documentation
docs.text:
We are using `mkdocs for the documentation. If you edit the documentation, you can build it locally. For this, you need to install an additional set of dependencies:

uvpip 
uv pip install -e ".[docs]"

pip install -e ".[docs]"

You can then serve the documentation locally by running

mkdocs serve

docs.title: Contributing to the package on GitHub
docs.text: Once you are happy with the changes, please commit them on a separate branch and create a pull request on GitHub. We run a number of GitHub actions that check the correct linting, run the tests in an isolated environment, and build the documentation. Once these pass and a peer review of the code has occurred, your code will be accepted.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If you face any issues with the tool or when setting up the development environment, please create a new Github Issue.

docs.title: What should you know before this tutorial?
docs.text:
To get started, it does not hurt to read the following pynxtools tutorial:

  • Guide on getting started with pynxtools, NeXus, and NOMAD
  • Installation tutorial for pynxtools

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text:
You will know

  • how to install pynxtools-xrd
  • how to install pynxtools-xrd together with NOMAD

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
It is recommended to use python 3.12 with a dedicated virtual environment for this package. Learn how to manage python versions and virtual environments.

There are many alternatives to managing virtual environments and package dependencies (requirements). We recommend using uv, an extremely fast Python package and project manager. In this tutorial, you will find parallel descriptions, using either uv or a more classical approach using venv and pip.

Start by creating a virtual environment:

uvvenv

uv is capable of creating a virtual environment and install the required Python version at the same time.

uv venv --python 3.12

Note that you will need to install the Python version manually beforehand.

python -m venv .venv

That command creates a new virtual environment in a directory called .venv.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install the latest stable version of this package from PyPI with

uvpip 
uv pip install pynxtools-xrd

pip install pynxtools-xrd

You can also install the latest development version with

uvpip 
uv pip install git+https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-xrd.git

pip install git+https://github.com/FAIRmat-NFDI/pynxtools-xrd.git

docs.title: How to install pynxtools-xrd with NOMAD
docs.text: To use pynxtools-xrd with NOMAD, simply install it in the same environment as the nomad-lab package. NOMAD will recognize pynxtools (and it pynxtools-xrd reader plugin) as a plugin automatically. In addition, NOMAD will install a schema for NeXus application definitions.

docs.title: Start using pynxtools-xrd
docs.text: That's it! You can now use pynxtools-xrd!

docs.title: Convert X-ray powder diffraction data and metadata to NeXus
Who is this tutorial for?
docs.text: This document is for X-ray diffraction scientists who want to standardize their research data by converting these into a NeXus standardized format.

docs.title: What should you know before this tutorial?
docs.text: • You should have a basic understanding of NeXus and FAIRmat's contribution to NeXus and pynxtools

docs.title: What you will know at the end of this tutorial?
docs.text: You will have a basic understanding how to use pynxtools-xrd for converting XRD data to a NeXus/HDF5 file.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: See the installation tutorial for how to install pynxtools together with the XRD reader plugin.

docs.title: Running the reader from the command line
docs.text:
An example script to run the XRD reader in pynxtools:

user@box:~$ dataconverter $<xrd-file path> $<eln-file path> --reader xrd --nxdl NXxrd_pan --output <output-file path>.nxs

You can find an example .xrdml file in tests/data/xrdml_918-16_10.

Congrats! You now have a NeXus file!
